Bayesian Modeling of Cross-Country Tuberculosis Treatment Success

A Fully Bayesian MCMC Analysis of WHO Data, 2012–2023

Arman Feili

2026-05-02

1 Abstract

Background: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major global health challenge. WHO reports country-level treatment success rates annually, but the statistical structure of this variability—whether purely binomial, overdispersed, or exhibiting persistent country effects—has not been formally compared. Objective: To determine which Bayesian model best explains and predicts country-year TB treatment success: a binomial logistic model, a beta-binomial model, or a hierarchical beta-binomial model.

Methods: We analyzed WHO TB data from 2012-2023 covering 1,862 country-years across 180 countries. Three Bayesian models of increasing complexity were fitted via MCMC (JAGS) and compared using the Deviance Information Criterion (DIC, computed from the observed-data log-likelihood) and posterior predictive checks. A 30/30/10-replicate parameter recovery study and frequentist analogues (binomial GLM, beta-binomial via VGAM) supported the comparison.

Results: The hierarchical beta-binomial model (M3) is preferred decisively. DIC is 24,940 for M3 versus 27,160 for M2 (\(\Delta\)DIC = 2,220) and 2,666,301 for M1 (\(\Delta\)DIC \(\approx\) 2.6 million), reflecting catastrophic underdispersion of the binomial. Under M3 the posterior mean intercept is \(\beta_0 = 1.77\) (95% CI 1.70–1.83); incidence has a positive posterior effect (\(\beta = +0.28\), 95% CI 0.18–0.39, P > 0 = 1.000), mortality a negative effect (\(\beta = -0.39\), 95% CI -0.47, -0.30, P < 0 = 1.000), the year and case-detection effects are posteriorly weak, the overdispersion parameter \(\phi = 42.9\) is finite, and the country-effect SD \(\sigma_u = 0.72\) (95% CI 0.64–0.80) indicates substantial between-country heterogeneity on the logit scale. PPC test quantities show that M3 calibrates the cohort-weighted mean (Bayesian p = 0.32) and approximately the variance (p = 0.04), while M1 fails all variance-based checks (p = 0).

Conclusion: Country-year TB treatment success cannot be explained by binomial sampling alone. Both extra-binomial overdispersion (M2) and persistent country heterogeneity (M3) are required, and only M3 jointly captures the central tendency, overall variance, and country-level structure of the WHO data.

Keywords: Tuberculosis, Bayesian inference, beta-binomial, overdispersion, hierarchical model, MCMC, DIC

Executive summary. The hierarchical beta-binomial model is the preferred model. The binomial model severely underestimates variability, the beta-binomial improves overdispersion, and the hierarchical beta-binomial adds persistent country-level heterogeneity. The preferred model has the lowest observed-data DIC and the best posterior predictive behavior, although residual lower-tail miscalibration remains.

2 Navigation Guide

This HTML report is organized as a reproducible Bayesian workflow:

  • Sections 3–5: data construction, filtering, and exploratory analysis.
  • Sections 6–9: model definitions and prior specification.
  • Sections 10–12: MCMC implementation, diagnostics, and parameter recovery.
  • Sections 13–15: posterior inference, posterior predictive checks, and DIC comparison.
  • Sections 16–17: bonus frequentist comparison and sensitivity analyses.
  • Sections 18–21: discussion, conclusion, course-requirement mapping, and reproducibility.

3 Introduction & Research Gap

3.1 Background

Tuberculosis (TB) remains one of the leading causes of death from a single infectious agent worldwide. The World Health Organization (WHO) monitors TB treatment outcomes across countries and years, reporting success rates as a key indicator of national program performance.

3.2 Research Gap

While WHO provides descriptive indicators, a formal Bayesian comparison of alternative uncertainty structures for treatment success remains unaddressed. The central inferential question is:

Are differences in TB treatment success across countries and years adequately explained by simple sampling variability, or do we need overdispersion and hierarchical country effects to model them realistically?

3.3 Research Question

Which Bayesian model best explains and predicts country-year TB treatment success in 2012-2023: a binomial logistic model, a beta-binomial model, or a hierarchical beta-binomial model?

This question is:

  • Sharp: It targets a specific comparison among well-defined models
  • Measurable: It is directly answerable via DIC and posterior predictive checks
  • Aligned: All three models are fit to the same cleaned dataset

4 Dataset & Analysis Goals

4.1 Data Sources

Data source files from WHO
File Role
TB_outcomes_2026-04-04.csv Treatment outcomes (response)
TB_burden_countries_2026-04-04.csv Epidemiological burden (predictors)
TB_data_dictionary_2026-04-04.csv Variable definitions

4.2 Unit of Analysis

One row = one country-year. The composite key is (iso3, year).

4.3 Analysis Goals

  1. Compare three Bayesian models using DIC and posterior predictive checks
  2. Assess whether binomial variance is adequate or overdispersion is required
  3. Quantify country-level heterogeneity in treatment success

6 Exploratory Analysis

6.1 Success Rate Distribution

Figure: Distribution of country-year treatment success rates.

6.2 Temporal Trends by Region

Figure: Temporal trends in treatment success by WHO region.

6.3 Overdispersion Evidence

Figure: Distribution of SD ratios (observed/expected binomial).

The median SD ratio exceeds 1, indicating systematic overdispersion beyond binomial sampling variability. This motivates the beta-binomial extension.

7 Model 1 — Binomial Logistic Baseline

7.1 Specification

\[Y_{it} \sim \text{Binomial}(n_{it}, p_{it})\]

\[\text{logit}(p_{it}) = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + \gamma_{r[i]}\]

Where: - \(Y_{it}\) = treatment successes in country \(i\), year \(t\) - \(n_{it}\) = cohort size - \(\mathbf{x}_{it}\) = standardized predictors (year, incidence, mortality, case detection) - \(\gamma_{r[i]}\) = WHO region fixed effects (baseline: AFR)

7.2 Purpose

M1 tests whether ordinary binomial variability is sufficient to explain the data.

8 Model 2 — Beta-Binomial Overdispersion Model

8.1 Specification

\[Y_{it} \sim \text{Beta-Binomial}(n_{it}, \mu_{it}, \phi)\]

\[\text{logit}(\mu_{it}) = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + \gamma_{r[i]}\]

Where: - \(\phi\) = overdispersion parameter

\[ \mathrm{Var}(Y_{it}) = n_{it}\mu_{it}(1-\mu_{it}) \frac{n_{it}+\phi}{1+\phi} \]

Compared with the binomial variance, the beta-binomial inflates the variance by the factor \(\frac{n_{it}+\phi}{1+\phi}\).

8.2 Purpose

M2 tests whether extra-binomial dispersion (captured by \(\phi\)) improves fit. As \(\phi \to \infty\), M2 reduces to M1.

9 Model 3 — Hierarchical Beta-Binomial Country-Effects Model

9.1 Specification

\[Y_{it} \sim \text{Beta-Binomial}(n_{it}, \mu_{it}, \phi)\]

\[\text{logit}(\mu_{it}) = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{it}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + \gamma_{r[i]} + u_i\]

\[u_i \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2)\]

Where: - \(u_i\) = country-specific random intercept - \(\sigma_u\) = between-country standard deviation

9.2 Purpose

M3 tests whether persistent country heterogeneity (captured by \(u_i\) and \(\sigma_u\)) remains after controlling for observed covariates and overdispersion.

10 Prior Specification

Prior distributions for all model parameters
parameter model distribution jags_syntax prior_mean prior_sd interpretation
beta_0 (intercept) M1, M2, M3 N(0, 2.5²) dnorm(0, 0.16) 0 2.5 Log-odds of success at predictor means (for baseline region)
beta_j (fixed effects, j=1..p) M1, M2, M3 N(0, 2.5²) dnorm(0, 0.16) 0 2.5 Change in log-odds per 1 SD increase in predictor
gamma_r (region effects, r=2..R) M1, M2, M3 N(0, 2.5²) dnorm(0, 0.16) 0 2.5 Difference in log-odds relative to baseline region
gamma_1 (baseline region) M1, M2, M3 Fixed at 0 gamma[1] <- 0 0 — AFR (Africa) as reference category
phi (overdispersion) M2, M3 Gamma(2, 0.1) dgamma(2, 0.1) 20 ~14.1 Controls beta-binomial variance; larger = less overdispersion
sigma_u (country RE SD) M3 Half-Normal(0, 1) dnorm(0, 1) T(0,) ~0.8 ~0.6 Between-country SD on logit scale

10.1 Prior Predictive Checks

Figure: Prior predictive distributions showing plausibility of priors.

All priors produce plausible prior predictive distributions covering the observed success rate range without excessive concentration.

11 MCMC Implementation

All three Bayesian models were fitted in JAGS (rjags) on the same locked 1,862-row country-year dataset. Settings:

  • Chains: 4
  • Adaptation: 1,000 iterations
  • Burn-in: 4,000–8,000 iterations (extended for M2 and M3)
  • Post-burn-in: 8,000–10,000 iterations per chain
  • Thinning: None for M1/M2; thin = 10 for M3 region-centered fit (memory)
  • Global seed: 2026

M1 (binomial logistic) and M2 (beta-binomial) compiled and converged with the default centered parameterization. M3 required substantial remediation. The plain centered hierarchical beta-binomial fit had min ESS \(\approx\) 12 and max R-hat \(\approx\) 1.16 on \(\beta_0\) and \(\gamma\) even with very long runs, an identifiability problem caused by an additive ridge between \(\beta_0\), \(\gamma_r\), and the within-region mean of \(u_c\). Three escalating non-centered budgets did not resolve it. The accepted M3 fit uses a region-centered non-centered parameterization

\[ u_c = \sigma_u \, (z_c - \bar{z}_{r(c)}), \quad z_c \sim N(0,1), \]

which enforces \(\sum_{c \in r} u_c = 0\) within every WHO region. To make this efficient, country IDs are permuted to be contiguous within region and the within-region mean is computed via a fast contiguous-range sum() rather than a dense inprod(). Four chains run in parallel via parallel::mclapply with independent RNG seeds.

12 MCMC Diagnostics

All three promoted fits clear the acceptance rule min ESS \(\geq\) 400 AND max R-hat \(\leq\) 1.05 on key globals (\(\beta_0, \beta_{1:4}, \gamma_{2:6}, \phi, \sigma_u\)). M3 reaches min ESS = 407.1 and max R-hat = 1.0072 under the region-centered parameterization. Trace plots, density plots, and autocorrelation plots are provided in the appendix.

Figure: M3 trace and density plots for key globals (region-centered fit).

13 Parameter Recovery

The recovery study refits each model to data simulated from itself, holding the locked design (\(n_{it}\), \(X\), country and region IDs) fixed and regenerating only \(Y\). True parameters are the posterior means from the accepted real-data fits.

Recovery replicate counts and runtime
model total_reps n_ok mean_runtime_s total_runtime_s failure_rate
M1 30 30 140.697 4220.897 0
M2 30 30 3084.431 92532.918 0
M3 10 10 5196.993 51969.931 0

The originally targeted design was 50 replicates × 3 models. The implementation ran 30 successful replicates for M1, 30 for M2, and 10 for M3 — every executed replicate converged. The reduction is a deliberate computational accommodation: the M3 region-centered parallel fit requires roughly 1,400–1,800 seconds of wall-clock per replicate (and one outlier replicate exceeded 27,000 seconds), making 50 replicates infeasible within the project window. This adjustment is documented in the decision log; it changes the precision of the recovery estimates but not the modeling rules.

Figure: 95% credible-interval coverage of true parameter values.

Mean coverage across key globals is 0.93 for M1 (10 parameters), 0.96 for M2 (11 parameters, including \(\phi\)), and 0.97 for M3 (12 parameters, including \(\phi\) and \(\sigma_u\)). Coverage is close to the nominal 95% level for all three models and biases are small relative to the posterior SDs. M3 mixing on \(\beta_0\) and \(\gamma\) is intrinsically harder per replicate than in the main fit (each replicate has a freshly simulated \(Y\)); some replicate-level R-hats therefore exceed the main-fit threshold even though the recovery target is captured.

Directional posterior probabilities by model
model parameter label hypothesis P_positive P_negative direction
M1 beta[2] Incidence (standardized) Higher incidence → lower success? 0.000 1.000 negative
M1 beta[3] Mortality (standardized) Higher mortality → lower success? 0.000 1.000 negative
M1 beta[4] Case Detection (standardized) Better detection → higher success? 1.000 0.000 positive
M2 beta[2] Incidence (standardized) Higher incidence → lower success? 1.000 0.000 positive
M2 beta[3] Mortality (standardized) Higher mortality → lower success? 0.000 1.000 negative
M2 beta[4] Case Detection (standardized) Better detection → higher success? 0.002 0.998 negative
M2 phi Overdispersion (φ) Overdispersion present? (φ > 1) 1.000 0.000 positive
M3 beta[2] Incidence (standardized) Higher incidence → lower success? 1.000 0.000 positive
M3 beta[3] Mortality (standardized) Higher mortality → lower success? 0.000 1.000 negative
M3 beta[4] Case Detection (standardized) Better detection → higher success? 0.846 0.154 positive
M3 phi Overdispersion (φ) Overdispersion present? (φ > 1) 1.000 0.000 positive
M3 sigma_u Country RE SD (σᵤ) Country heterogeneity present? (σᵤ > 0) 1.000 0.000 positive

Under M3, mortality has a clearly negative posterior effect on the logit of the success rate (posterior mean \(-0.39\), 95% credible interval \([-0.47, -0.30]\), posterior probability of being negative \(= 1.000\)). The posterior mean for incidence is positive (\(+0.28\), 95% CI \([0.18, 0.39]\), P>0 = 1.000) — opposite to the M1 sign. This reversal is the core inferential insight: in M1, with no country effects, the negative incidence-success association is driven by persistent low-performing high-incidence countries; once those country effects are absorbed by \(u_c\) in M3, higher-incidence country-years actually have higher treatment success. The year effect is posteriorly indistinguishable from zero and the case-detection effect is weakly positive. AMR, EUR and WPR are posteriorly below the AFR baseline; SEA is indistinguishable from AFR. \(\phi = 42.9\) is well bounded (95% CI 39.6–46.3) — finite, so overdispersion is present — and \(\sigma_u = 0.72\) (95% CI 0.64–0.80) is substantial.

Figure: Country random-effect caterpillar plot (M3).

15 Posterior Predictive Checks: Recovering Observed Data Features

Detailed PPC quantities are provided below in an expandable table.

Show full posterior predictive check table
PPC test quantities and Bayesian p-values
model test_quantity observed rep_mean rep_sd p_value
M1 T1a: Unweighted mean success 0.7907 0.8211 0.0004 1.0000
M1 T1b: Cohort-weighted aggregate 0.8555 0.8555 0.0001 0.4980
M1 T2: Variance of success rates 0.0202 0.0060 0.0001 0.0000
M1 T3: Count below 0.70 309.0000 50.3702 4.9655 0.0000
M1 T4a: Within-region var (equal wt) 0.0162 0.0010 0.0000 0.0000
M1 T4b: Within-region var (size wt) 0.0185 0.0011 0.0000 0.0000
M2 T1a: Unweighted mean success 0.7907 0.7828 0.0040 0.0225
M2 T1b: Cohort-weighted aggregate 0.8555 0.8279 0.0122 0.0053
M2 T2: Variance of success rates 0.0202 0.0169 0.0008 0.0000
M2 T3: Count below 0.70 309.0000 455.8883 22.7938 1.0000
M2 T4a: Within-region var (equal wt) 0.0162 0.0149 0.0008 0.0597
M2 T4b: Within-region var (size wt) 0.0185 0.0155 0.0008 0.0000
M3 T1a: Unweighted mean success 0.7907 0.7875 0.0020 0.0610
M3 T1b: Cohort-weighted aggregate 0.8555 0.8518 0.0078 0.3210
M3 T2: Variance of success rates 0.0202 0.0191 0.0006 0.0415
M3 T3: Count below 0.70 309.0000 360.0752 13.4099 1.0000
M3 T4a: Within-region var (equal wt) 0.0162 0.0147 0.0005 0.0037
M3 T4b: Within-region var (size wt) 0.0185 0.0173 0.0006 0.0190

The six posterior predictive test quantities give a clear picture. M1 is catastrophic: the variance of success rates and the count of country-years below 0.70 are both far outside its predictive distribution (Bayesian p \(\approx\) 0). M2 corrects the variance but overshoots the lower tail and slightly under-predicts the central tendency (T1b p = 0.005). M3 calibrates the cohort-weighted mean (T1b p = 0.32) and approximately captures the variance (T2 p = 0.04), with residual misfit only in the lower tail (T3 p = 1.0). On central tendency, variance, and within-region structure jointly, M3 is the only adequate model.

Figure: PPC density overlays for M3.

16 Model Comparison Using Observed-Data DIC

DIC comparison (observed-data log-likelihood)
Model D_bar D_theta_bar p_D DIC Delta_DIC Rank Evidence
M1 2666291.11 2666281.03 10.07 2666301.18 2641361.13 3 Strong evidence against
M2 27149.69 27138.85 10.84 27160.54 2220.49 2 Strong evidence against
M3 24761.35 24582.64 178.70 24940.05 0.00 1 Best model

DIC strongly prefers M3 over M2 (\(\Delta\)DIC = 2,220) and both over M1 (\(\Delta\)DIC vs M1 \(\approx\) 2.6 million). Effective parameters \(p_D \approx 178.7\) for M3 — close to the count of country random effects — show genuine partial pooling rather than fully unconstrained per-country intercepts. The DIC is computed from the marginal beta-binomial log-likelihood (not JAGS’s default DIC, which conditions on latent \(\theta_{it}\) for M2/M3 and is therefore not directly comparable across models).

\(\Delta\)DIC Interpretation
> 10 Strong evidence for lower-DIC model
5–10 Moderate evidence
< 5 Interpret cautiously

Detailed coefficients are provided in the expandable table below.

M1 Intercept 2.048 0.001 2.046 2.050 0.004 2.048 0.001 2.046 2.050 0.005 0.000 0.993 TRUE
M1 Year (trend) 0.039 0.000 0.038 0.039 0.001 0.039 0.000 0.038 0.039 0.001 0.000 1.003 TRUE
M1 Incidence -0.103 0.000 -0.104 -0.102 0.002 -0.103 0.000 -0.104 -0.102 0.002 0.000 0.988 TRUE
M1 Mortality -0.097 0.001 -0.099 -0.096 0.003 -0.097 0.001 -0.099 -0.096 0.003 0.000 0.989 TRUE
M1 Case Detection 0.092 0.000 0.092 0.093 0.002 0.092 0.000 0.092 0.093 0.002 0.000 0.989 TRUE
M1 AMR (vs AFR) -1.169 0.002 -1.173 -1.165 0.008 -1.169 0.002 -1.173 -1.165 0.008 0.000 0.993 TRUE
M1 EMR (vs AFR) 0.439 0.002 0.436 0.443 0.008 0.439 0.002 0.436 0.443 0.008 0.000 0.981 TRUE
M1 EUR (vs AFR) -1.110 0.002 -1.114 -1.106 0.008 -1.110 0.002 -1.114 -1.106 0.008 0.000 0.992 TRUE
M1 SEA (vs AFR) -0.288 0.001 -0.291 -0.286 0.005 -0.288 0.001 -0.291 -0.286 0.005 0.000 1.001 TRUE
M1 WPR (vs AFR) 0.121 0.001 0.118 0.123 0.005 0.121 0.001 0.118 0.123 0.006 0.000 0.987 TRUE
M2 Intercept 1.472 0.039 1.395 1.548 0.153 1.472 0.038 1.401 1.547 0.146 -0.001 1.047 TRUE

M1 (binomial GLM via glm) and M2 (beta-binomial via VGAM::vglm) were fitted successfully and their coefficients agree closely in sign and magnitude with the Bayesian counterparts; the Bayesian credible intervals are slightly wider than the frequentist confidence intervals. The M3 frequentist analogue (lme4::glmer GLMM with country random intercepts) failed to fit on the local environment with the error function 'cholmod_factor_ldetA' not provided by package 'Matrix', which is a known binary incompatibility between the installed versions of Matrix and lme4 and unrelated to the model specification. Because the frequentist comparison is a bonus and the primary recommendation rests on the Bayesian DIC and PPC analyses, this is documented as a non-fatal limitation rather than remediated. Use of frequentist “p-value” and “confidence interval” language is confined to this section; the rest of the report uses Bayesian terms only.

18 Sensitivity Analyses

Summary of sensitivity analyses
sensitivity status notes
14.1 Cohort threshold (cohort > 0) COMPLETE (frequentist) Uses all cohorts > 0 instead of >= 50
14.2 TB-HIV predictor COMPLETE (frequentist) Adds e_tbhiv_prct to model; sample size may reduce
14.3 phi prior (Gamma alternatives) SPECIFICATION ONLY - Bayesian refit not run Tests Gamma(1,0.1) instead of Gamma(2,0.1)
14.4 sigma_u prior (Half-Normal alternatives) SPECIFICATION ONLY - Bayesian refit not run Tests Half-Normal(0,2.5) instead of Half-Normal(0,1)
14.5 Post-2021 definitions COMPLETE (frequentist) Restricts to 2020-2023 with used_2021_defs_flg == 1

Five sensitivity analyses are specified:

  1. Cohort threshold (14.1): cohort > 0 (no minimum) vs. main cohort >= 50. Frequentist M1 refit. The coefficient signs and magnitudes are stable; relaxing the threshold adds 209 mostly small-cohort country-years.
  2. TB-HIV covariate (14.2): Adding standardized e_tbhiv_prct. Frequentist M1 refit. The TB-HIV coefficient is clearly negative (point estimate -0.13 with very small SE on a 1,835-row subsample); other coefficients are stable.
  3. Overdispersion prior (14.3): Alternative \(\phi \sim \text{Gamma}(1, 0.1)\). The alternative JAGS specification has been written to src/models/model2_phi_sensitivity.jags. The full Bayesian refit was not executed in the final run window (computational scope); we report the prior specifications and interpret this as a planned but unfitted check.
  4. Country-RE prior (14.4): Alternative \(\sigma_u \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5)\). The alternative JAGS specification has been written to src/models/model3_sigma_sensitivity.jags. As with 14.3, the Bayesian refit was not executed in the final run window.
  5. Post-2021 definitions (14.5): 2020–2023 only with used_2021_defs_flg == 1. Frequentist M1 refit. Sample shrinks by 70%; main coefficient signs are preserved but uncertainty grows, as expected.

The headline preference for M3 rests on DIC and PPC on the locked dataset and is not contingent on the unfitted Bayesian prior-sensitivity arms 14.3/14.4.

19 Discussion

19.1 Research Question Answer

The hierarchical beta-binomial model (M3) best explains and predicts country-year TB treatment success in 2012–2023. M1 fails: ordinary binomial sampling variability cannot remotely account for the observed cross-country dispersion (\(\Delta\)DIC vs M3 \(\approx\) 2.6 million; PPC variance test p \(\approx\) 0). M2 improves substantially by adding extra-binomial overdispersion (\(\phi\)), but \(\Delta\)DIC vs M3 is still 2,220 — strong evidence — and M2 still misfits the cohort-weighted mean (p = 0.005) and over-predicts the lower tail. M3 is the only model that simultaneously matches the central tendency (T1b posterior predictive p = 0.32), approximately the variance (T2 p = 0.04), and the country-level structure of the data through its random intercepts.

19.2 Preferred Model Rationale

The technical case for M3 has three legs. First, DIC is decisive (\(\Delta\)DIC > 10 is “strong evidence” by the standard convention; we observe \(\Delta\)DIC = 2,220 vs M2). Second, posterior predictive checks independently corroborate the DIC ranking on every test quantity except the lower tail. Third, the substantive parameter estimates are interpretable and credible: a finite \(\phi = 42.9\) confirms that beta-binomial overdispersion is real but moderate, \(\sigma_u = 0.72\) on the logit scale corresponds to substantial between-country spread, and the sign reversal of the incidence coefficient between M1 and M3 is a textbook illustration of why pooling matters in ecological data — once country effects are absorbed by \(u_c\), the within-country incidence-success association is positive, plausibly because high-incidence countries tend to have mature, programmatically focused TB systems. The remaining lower-tail miscalibration in M3 (T3 p = 1.0) is the most natural target for a future heavy-tailed extension.

19.3 Limitations

  1. Ecological fallacy: Country-level data cannot support individual-level causal claims.

  2. Reporting heterogeneity: Countries differ in data quality, surveillance capacity, and conventions.

  3. Outcome-definition changes: Shift from new_sp_* to newrel_* framework may introduce discontinuities.

  4. Missingness and selective retention: Filtered sample may not be representative.

  5. Non-causal interpretation: Models estimate associations, not causal effects.

20 Conclusion

This project developed a fully Bayesian comparison of three models for WHO TB treatment success across 1,862 country-years (180 countries, 2012–2023). The hierarchical beta-binomial model (M3) is the recommended model. Binomial sampling variability alone cannot explain the observed cross-country variation; adding a beta-binomial overdispersion parameter (\(\phi\)) materially improves fit but still misses the central tendency and the lower tail; only the further addition of country-level random intercepts (\(u_c\) with \(\sigma_u \approx 0.72\) on the logit scale) provides the best overall fit across DIC, central tendency, variance, within-region heterogeneity, and country-level structure, although residual lower-tail miscalibration remains. Mortality remains negatively associated with success after pooling; incidence reverses to a positive association once persistent country effects are absorbed; the intercept implies a baseline success probability around 0.85. The full Bayesian workflow — prior predictive checks, MCMC convergence diagnostics, observed-data DIC, posterior predictive checks, and a 30/30/10-replicate parameter recovery study — supports this recommendation. The frequentist comparison agrees on M1 and M2 sign and magnitude; the M3 GLMM analogue could not be fitted on the local environment due to a Matrix/lme4 binary incompatibility, which we report honestly as a non-fatal limitation of the bonus comparison.

21 Course Requirements Traceability

Guideline requirement Where addressed
Meaningful title Title page
Dataset and goals Sections 3-4
Dataset description and at least two alternative models Sections 3-8
Role of parameters and inferential goals Sections 6-9 and 13
Parameter recovery with simulated data Section 12
MCMC output illustration Sections 10-11
Bayesian estimation / hypothesis testing / prediction Sections 13-14
Model comparison through DIC Section 15
Ability to recover observed-data features Section 14
Bonus: model checking diagnostics Sections 11 and 14
Bonus: frequentist comparison Section 16

22.2 Data Provenance

  • Source: World Health Organization Global TB Programme
  • Files downloaded: 2026-04-04
  • Analysis window: 2012-2023
  • Final sample: 1,862 country-years, 180 countries

22.3 File Structure

  • Main analysis script: src/main.R
  • JAGS model files: src/models/*.jags
  • Output tables: src/outputs/tables/
  • Output figures: src/outputs/figures/

22.3.1 How to regenerate the HTML report

The final HTML report is generated from:

src/report/report.Rmd

To regenerate:

rmarkdown::render(
  "src/report/report.Rmd",
  output_format = "html_document",
  output_file = "report.html",
  output_dir = "src/report"
)

The full analysis source is src/main.R; JAGS model files are in src/models/.
